topic: A person's longtime hobby becomes their child's obsession
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the worn grooves of the mahogany desk, the familiar scent of aged wood suddenly suffocating. For thirty years, woodworking had been his sanctuary, a quiet place where the chaos of the outside world dissolved into the satisfying click of a plane against oak. But now, the sanctuary felt invaded. Leo, his seven-year-old son, no longer asked to watch him carve; he demanded to *be* the carver. Every afternoon, the workshop, once a place of peaceful creation, was now a high-stakes arena. Leo would hover, his small fingers twitching with an intense, almost feverish focus, watching Elias’s precise movements with wide, unblinking eyes. When Elias finally managed a smooth curve, Leo would gasp, a sound of profound, almost desperate awe. Elias would try to maintain his composure, forcing a smile that felt brittle on his face, but the tightness in his chest made it impossible. He found himself resenting the intensity of the child’s gaze, the way Leo’s entire being seemed to revolve around the grain of the wood. Sometimes, when Leo tried to mimic the cuts, the result was clumsy and jagged, and the resulting sigh from Elias was heavy with unspoken weariness.

[story 2]

"Just a little deeper, Leo. Feel the resistance," Elias murmured, his voice pitched low, trying to inject calm into the humming tension of the afternoon. He watched his son’s tiny hands grip the chisel, knuckles white against the metal. The air in the workshop felt thick, charged with expectation. It wasn't just a hobby anymore; it was a performance, and Elias was the unwilling director. He noticed the way Leo’s breath hitched before a difficult stroke, the minute adjustments in his posture as he tried to match the elder craftsman's technique. A cold knot tightened in Elias’s stomach every time Leo’s concentration narrowed to a pinprick. He wanted to step back, to simply clean the tools and retreat to the quiet solitude he craved, but he couldn't. If he stopped, Leo would lose his focus, and the resulting frustration radiating off the boy would feel like a physical pressure against Elias’s ribs. He picked up a piece of maple, the smooth surface offering no solace, only the mirror of his own mounting strain.

[story 3]

I remember the sound of the sandpaper—a rhythmic, grating whisper that used to soothe my nerves. Now, it’s a metronome counting down to some impossible standard. My son, Finn, has taken my lifelong passion for miniature ship modeling and elevated it to an obsessive quest. He doesn't just want to assemble the kits; he wants to replicate the exact patina, the microscopic imperfections on the rigging, down to the very grain of the tiny masts. When he sits beside me, his brow furrowed in deep concentration, his small hands delicately applying a thin coat of varnish, I feel a strange sense of dread mixed with a strange pride. It’s the sheer, unrelenting detail that wears me down. I find myself constantly second-guessing my own technique, scrutinizing every joint, terrified that one misplaced bead of glue will shatter the meticulous world Finn is building. There are days when I just stare at the completed models—perfect, tiny worlds—and feel a hollow ache, realizing that the joy of creation has morphed into a relentless obligation to meet a standard only a child can set.
